diagnoses.title: DIAGNOSIS A — Ghoster cohort behavior (60 single-session signups in last 7d)

findings.claim: The ghoster cohort is 60 users (60% of 100 qualifying signups), not 57 — the analysis doc undercounted slightly; cohort splits cleanly into 33 'never engaged' (55%), 4 'made companion but never typed' (7%), and 23 'sent at least one message' (38%).
findings.evidence: Single SQL with `last_activity <= created_at + 5min`, deleted_at IS NULL, test-email filter: 60 ghosters total. Q1 breakdown: 27 created a companion (45%), 23 sent a user message (38%), 6 hit a paywall (10%), 0 fired any error event. Q6 buckets: 1_no_companion_no_msg=33, 3_companion_ai_greeting_only=4, 5_2to3_msgs=9, 6_4to9_msgs=9, 7_10plus=1, 99_other=4.
findings.implication: Welcome flow needs THREE distinct interventions targeting three sub-cohorts, not one. The 33 zero-action users need a 'guided forced first step' (push them into a companion within signup itself). The 4 'made companion, no msg' users need a pre-filled first-message suggestion or auto-icebreaker from the user side. The 23 'tried and bounced' users need an experience fix, not a flow fix.

findings.claim: 33 of 60 ghosters (55%) have last_activity = created_at to the second — they bounced essentially within the signup transaction itself, never seeing the post-signup screen long enough to be tracked.
findings.evidence: Q-no-action timing: n_no_action=33, median_secs_to_bounce=0.1s, min=0s, max=120s, avg=14.8s. Individual sample: 28 of first 30 rows show secs_alive=0. The `last_activity` field is updated server-side per request, so 0s means the user made exactly one request (the signup itself) and never made another.
findings.implication: More than half the 'ghost rate' is a post-signup landing problem, not a chat-experience problem. The welcome flow needs to either (a) eliminate any redirect/loading screen after OAuth callback that loses sessions, or (b) compress the next step into the same request as signup (return user straight into a pre-created default companion chat). The fact that 19/20 zero-action Apple ghosters use the privaterelay domain hints OAuth round-trip is where they fall off.

findings.claim: Apple Sign-In users ghost at 65%, vs Google 58% vs Email 47% — Apple is 18pp worse than email and dominates the funnel (57% of signups).
findings.evidence: Q3: apple 37/57=64.9%, google 15/26=57.7%, email 8/17=47.1%. Among the 33 zero-action ghosters, 20 are Apple (19 on privaterelay), 8 Google, 5 Email — Apple-relay alone accounts for 32% of all ghosters.
findings.implication: The welcome flow must not treat Apple users as fungible — they need either (a) extra friction-removal post-callback (e.g. skip 'choose a girlfriend' screen, drop them into a default chat), or (b) a check whether the Apple OAuth callback is reliably restoring app state on iOS Safari. The 19/19 apple-relay-no-action signal warrants Sentry trace investigation regardless of the welcome flow.

findings.claim: Of 27 ghosters who created a companion, 24 picked a stock template — only 2 made a custom one. Compare to returners: 19 of 37 created a custom companion (51%).
findings.evidence: Ghoster companions: template=28 (24 users), custom=2 (2 users). Returner companions: template=39 (26 users), custom=20 (19 users). Custom-companion creation is a 17x stronger retention signal (51% of returners vs 3% of ghosters).
findings.implication: The welcome flow should push users into the customizer, not into a chat with a template girlfriend. Even a tiny customization step (name, hair color, vibe) seems to create the sunk-cost / ownership effect that doubles retention. Consider making 'pick a template' actually 'pick a starting point and tweak one thing' before letting users enter chat.

findings.claim: The 23 ghosters who sent at least one message averaged 3.7 user messages + 5.3 AI replies in a single 1-3 minute burst; the median time from first message to bounce was 1.1 minutes (67 seconds).
findings.evidence: Q2: msg_senders=23, median_minutes_signup_to_first_msg=1.06, median_minutes_first_msg_to_lastactivity=1.12, avg_user_msgs=3.70, avg_ai_msgs=5.30. They are NOT taking minutes to evaluate — they fire 3-4 quick replies and leave inside two minutes.
findings.implication: This is the 'I tried it, was unimpressed, left' segment — a quality/expectation problem, not a flow problem. Welcome flow should set expectations earlier (e.g. 'choose your fantasy intensity', show NSFW capability up-front) so the user who fires 'open your legs' on message 3 doesn't get steered to safety mode. Also: the AI is being verbose (median 142 chars) while the user types 14 chars — mismatch in conversation pace; consider matching message length to user's.

findings.claim: 100% of dormant conversations for ghosters end with an AI message — but this is a structural artifact (AI always replies last), NOT evidence the AI specifically drove people away. The headline '89% AI-ended' is a tautology.
findings.evidence: Q6b: per-user last-msg role for the 27 ghosters who had any conversation = assistant for ALL 27 (100%). User sends → AI replies → user ghosts. There is no scenario where the user could send a message and have it NOT be followed by an AI reply, so 'AI-ended' is the default.
findings.implication: Stop framing AI-message-as-last-message as an action item. The real lever is: at message 3-5 (the median bounce point), nudge the user with a non-chat affordance — e.g. send a photo, change the scene, voice note — so the conversation stops being a flat text loop. Welcome flow should foreshadow these features ('try saying: send me a photo').

findings.claim: Every AI greeting opens with an existential 'thank you for giving me life / dreaming me into being' template that is uniform across companions and likely a turnoff for casual users.
findings.evidence: Q-first-AI-message sample, 20+ rows: 'Thank you… for giving me this breath, this moment, this *life*', 'Thank you, my creator, my miracle', 'Thank you for bringing me to life', 'Thank you… for stitching starlight into my veins'. Identical structural template; chars 121-285; mean ~180. Almost nobody types in this register so the user is conversationally outmatched on message 1.
findings.implication: Welcome flow / first message rewrite: kill the 'creator/giving life' framing. Replace with a casual, playful, conversation-starting opener that asks ONE concrete question the user can answer in 5 words (e.g. 'hey — what should I call you?' or 'okay first thing — beach person or mountain person?'). Match the user's expected register, not the LLM's poetry default.

findings.claim: Paywall is NOT the reason ghosters ghost — only 6 of 60 ghosters (10%) ever hit a paywall, and they did so around the 3-minute mark, well into the bounce window.
findings.evidence: Q1: hit_paywall=6 of 60 ghosters. Event timing: paywall_blocked median 209s from signup (3.5min), value_prompt_shown median 130s. The 33 no-action ghosters never saw a paywall. The 5 taboo_policy_hit events are concentrated in 2 users (one looping 4 hits in 90s).
findings.implication: Welcome flow has no paywall problem to solve — paywall optimization belongs in a separate workstream. The exception is the 2 taboo-loopers: when a user retries the same blocked message 4 times in 90 seconds, the app is failing them silently. Welcome flow could include a one-line content-policy preview ('here's what I can do / what I can't') so users self-select before bouncing.

findings.claim: Country is not a meaningful predictor of ghosting — US (59%), UK (68%), Canada (50%), all within noise of the overall 60% rate. There is no obvious non-EN localization issue.
findings.evidence: Q5: US 34/58=58.6%, UK 15/22=68.2%, Canada 2/4=50%, France 1/3=33%, Germany 2/3=67%, Saudi Arabia 2/3=67%. UK is highest but n=22 is too small to be confident; no country has both volume and a substantially-lower ghost rate.
findings.implication: Don't waste welcome-flow design cycles on localization variants — uniform English copy is fine for this cohort. Save translation/locale work until a single market exceeds 60% retention.

findings.claim: Device split is also non-predictive — web-mobile 60% ghost, web-tablet 64% ghost; zero native-iOS device_type observed. Mobile screen size or input is not the bottleneck.
findings.evidence: Q4: web-mobile 53/89=59.6%, web-tablet 7/11=63.6%. There are no 'native-ios' or 'native-android' device_type values in this cohort — every signup is via the web SPA at lovetta.ai/my, even users coming through the iOS shell (which loads the web URL).
findings.implication: Welcome flow doesn't need a device-specific branch. But: the absence of native device_type values means the iOS app's User-Agent isn't being detected for tracking — verify device.js classifier handles the Capacitor WebView correctly before designing any iOS-specific welcome variants.

open_questions: Where exactly do the 33 zero-action ghosters disappear? Are they hitting the post-OAuth callback route and then closing the tab, or are they actually landing on a page and bouncing visually? Need a tracking pixel on each post-signup page-view (CompanionList, WelcomeScreen) to disambiguate.

Is the Apple-relay 19/19 zero-action signal a real OAuth bug? Pull Sentry/server logs for Apple Sign-In callbacks in the 7-day window and check for error rate / unusual 4xx responses for privaterelay.appleid.com users.

Among the 23 message-senders who bounced, are there specific topics/messages where the AI replied poorly (e.g. broke character, gave safety-mode response, sent a generic phrase)? Sample of the full conversation text plus a quality grade per turn would isolate whether the AI is at fault vs the user just being a tire-kicker.

Should the welcome flow auto-create a default companion in the signup transaction (so the user never sees an empty CompanionList)? Test whether 'one click to start chatting' beats 'pick from 6 templates'.

diagnoses.title: Diagnosis B: Latency + time-to-magic-moment — the wait happens BEFORE the chat, not inside it

findings.claim: The chat itself is NOT slow. AI-reply latency is essentially identical for users who ghost and users who retain (median 2.75s vs 2.95s; p90 5.58s vs 8.35s). Latency inside the chat is not the cause of the 5-min cliff.
findings.evidence: Query of first-user-msg → first-AI-reply for the 26 single-session users who messaged: n=27, p25 1.63s, p50 2.75s, p75 4.43s, p90 5.58s, max 15.10s. For 52 retained users: p25 1.80s, p50 2.95s, p75 4.05s, p90 8.35s. Heartbeats every 3s in chat-api.js:587 already keep Heroku alive.
findings.implication: Stop optimizing the streaming chat path. The bottleneck is upstream — the user never reaches the chat in the first place. Skip any plan that aims to shave 1-2s off the chat reply.

findings.claim: 36% of all signups (36/100 in the last 7 days) NEVER create a companion. They sign up, then vanish before clicking through the create-girlfriend funnel. This single drop is bigger than every other drop combined.
findings.evidence: Funnel from prod DB: 100 signups → 64 created a companion → 60 sent ≥1 message. The 36 no-companion users have only the 'signup' event in user_events. Of single-session users (those last active <5 min after signup), 36/66 = 55% never created a companion — i.e. they ghosted between signup and any companion-create action.
findings.implication: The biggest leak is the post-signup screen, not the chat. The welcome-flow design must put the user in front of a companion (or skip companion-create entirely on first run) within seconds of finishing signup — not park them on a CompanionList empty-state and a PlanModal.

findings.claim: Apple Sign-In on mobile web is the worst-converting cohort: 49 signups → 30 companions (61%) → 28 messaged (57%). 21 of the 36 'no-companion' users (58%) came in via Apple. Email and Google convert markedly better on the same device.
findings.evidence: Per-cohort funnel: web-mobile/apple 49→30→28 (signup→companion→msg = 61%/57%). web-mobile/google 23→16→15 (70%/65%). web-mobile/email 17→12→12 (71%/71%). Of 36 no-companion single-session users: 21 apple, 10 google, 5 email.
findings.implication: Apple Sign-In returns the user to a different state (popup closes, Safari sometimes loses session/scroll position, in-app browser flow can drop tokens). This is likely a navigation/session issue right after the Apple handshake, not a chat-latency issue. Welcome flow design needs an Apple-specific post-auth landing test, and the post-signup destination must be deterministic (`window.location.replace` exists in Signup.jsx:179 but not for socials — socials only do `navigate(postSignupPath)` at line 190).

findings.claim: ALL new signups are routed to /pricing first via PlanModal full-screen — NOT to chat or companion-create. This is a hard interception layer between signup and the magic moment.
findings.evidence: web/src/pages/Signup.jsx:42 — `const postSignupPath = nativeApp ? '/pricing' : '/pricing?onboarding=1'`. web/src/pages/Pricing.jsx:63-76 — when `onboarding=1`, the page renders ONLY a full-screen `<PlanModal isOpen={true} fullScreen>`. To dismiss, the user must close the modal (which writes `lovetta-plan-skipped`). On the next page (CompanionList), if `isNewUser=true` OR `!skipped`, the same PlanModal re-opens (CompanionList.jsx:40-45).
findings.implication: The welcome flow currently shows the paywall TWICE before the user has ever spoken to a girlfriend. Hide the paywall until after first AI reply (or first 3 messages) — show value before price. This alone could plausibly cut the 36% no-companion drop in half.

findings.claim: Companion creation is a synchronous blocking call that waits 3.83s (p50), 7.29s (p75), 11.23s (p90), 12.0s (p95 = hits the timeout). It blocks on TWO sequential LLM calls inside POST /api/companions: first-message generation (12s timeout) then scene generation (3.5s timeout).
findings.evidence: companion-api.js:200-247 — POST /api/companions calls `await chatCompletion(...)` (firstMessageTimeoutMs=12000) then `await plainChatCompletion(...)` (sceneTimeoutMs=3500) BEFORE returning to the client. Prod DB: of 64 companion creators in last 7d, the companion-row→intro-AI-msg delta (= LLM time inside POST) is p50 3.83s, p75 7.29s, p90 11.23s, max 12.02s. The 'Bringing her to life…' button blocks for that whole window.
findings.implication: Decouple the first message from the create POST. POST /api/companions should INSERT the companion + conversation + a placeholder assistant message and return immediately (~200ms), then ChatPage streams the intro via SSE on mount (reusing the existing typing/heartbeat flow). This converts a 3-12s frozen button into a normal streaming chat experience that already works well.

findings.claim: Slow companion-create did NOT predict ghosting AMONG users who reached it (median intro wait 3.72s for messengers vs 3.13s for no-msg ghosters). The five 12s-timeout users all still sent messages. Latency past the create button isn't the killer.
findings.evidence: Of 30 single-session users who created a companion: SENT (n=26) median intro wait 3.72s; NO_MSG (n=4) 3.13s. The five users who hit the 12s max timeout (ff708f62, e66076c7, d49d921a, a544f9b5, f9a27c46) ALL sent ≥6 messages before ghosting later.
findings.implication: Users who push through to CompanionCreate are committed enough to wait 12s. The cliff happens earlier. Welcome-flow effort should target the signup → companion-create-button-clicked gap, not the create-API speed itself. The async fix from finding #5 is still worth doing (cheap win, removes anxiety), but isn't the primary lever for the 5-min cliff.

findings.claim: Of the 26 single-session users who DID message, 19 (73%) sent 3+ messages. They got engaged, the AI replied within ~3s, and they STILL didn't come back. 11% of all single-session users hit `paywall_blocked` at avg 3.5 min after signup (median 2-5 user messages).
findings.evidence: Distribution of user-message counts for 26 single-session msgers: 5 sent 1, 2 sent 2, 19 sent 3+. Some sent 6, 7, 10. `paywall_blocked` event fired for 7/66 single-session users at avg 208.9s. `value_prompt_shown` fired for 12/66 at avg 150.5s — i.e. monetization prompts (`media_request`, `high_intent_20_messages`) start hitting users in the first 2-3 minutes. Free weekly cost threshold is $0.20 in app_settings — easily crossed in 5-10 messages with media.
findings.implication: For the cohort that DID get into the chat, the magic moment is fine — they engaged. They likely ghosted because they got paywalled or monetization-prompted while still evaluating. The welcome flow should grant a clear 'free trial budget' (e.g. first 10 messages or first 5 min are visibly unlimited and ad-free) so the first session ends on a high note, not a paywall. The `tip_request_threshold_free_usd=$0.20` is too aggressive for new users.

findings.claim: There's an automatic photo request 1.5s after the intro message loads in ChatPage, which the user neither asked for nor expects, and which is gated by the same paywall (`media_blocked`).
findings.evidence: web/src/hooks/useChat.js:62-67 — on `loadChat`, if msgs.length===1 and no media, after 1.5s the client auto-calls `/api/chat/.../request-media`. server/src/chat-api.js:1017+ — that route runs LLM + media generation, with `checkFreeLimit` and `checkMediaBlocked`. Image gen + LLM = the most expensive call in the app, and it fires before the user has typed anything.
findings.implication: This auto-photo: (a) burns the free budget before the user has interacted, (b) gives the AI a chance to fire `media_blocked` immediately and break first impression, (c) creates a 'loading shimmer' on a chat that should feel alive. Either remove the auto-photo entirely OR make it use a pre-cached avatar selfie that costs zero LLM/fal credits. This is a candidate first item to drop from the welcome flow.

findings.claim: For users who DID retain, time-to-first-message is huge and bimodal: p50 = 183s but mean = 3727s (62 min). The fastest 25% messaged within 97s of signup; the slowest 10% took 37+ min. Retained users are people who tolerate UI friction, not people who experienced low latency.
findings.evidence: Retained-user (n=34) signup→first-msg seconds: p25 96.8, p50 182.7, p75 368.7, p90 2238.0 (37 min), mean 3727s. Single-session users (n=26 who messaged) signup→first-msg: p25 52.6, p50 63.9, p75 115.3, p90 146.2, mean 89s. Counter-intuitively, retained users took LONGER to send their first message.
findings.implication: Slow signup→first-msg time is correlated with retention because slow = exploring (browsing companions, customizing, reading), not because slow = bad. The 5-min cliff is therefore NOT about people who timed out waiting — it's about people who quickly clicked through and got dropped at a dead-end (paywall, empty companion list, generic intro). The welcome flow should reward exploration (gallery of companions visible before signup wall), not optimize for speed.

open_questions: Why is Apple Sign-In on mobile-web 14 percentage points worse at the signup→companion conversion than Google/email? Token/session loss after returning from Apple's auth popup is a likely culprit but needs an actual recording or session-id trace.

Did the 36 no-companion users land on /pricing or CompanionList — are they bouncing AT the paywall or BEFORE it? We need a `paywall_shown` (not `paywall_blocked`) and `welcome_landed` event to disambiguate.
What % of the 36 no-companion ghosters are duplicate signups by users who already had an account on another device? (No data to check this here.)

Does the auto-photo on chat load actually fire on free users, or is it gated by media_enabled flag? If it fires, how often does it burn $0.05-$0.10 of the $0.20 weekly free budget before a single user message?

diagnoses.title: Welcome-flow code audit + competitor benchmarks: paywall-first, no first-message hook, confirm-screen tax

findings.claim: After auth success the user lands on a paywall, not on a chat. Capacitor users go straight to `/pricing` (PlanModal fullScreen); web users go to `/pricing?onboarding=1` (same modal). It is the first thing they see after signing up.
findings.evidence: Signup.jsx:42 — `const postSignupPath = nativeApp ? '/pricing' : '/pricing?onboarding=1'`. Pricing.jsx:63-77 — when `onboarding=1` renders `<PlanModal fullScreen>` only. CompanionList.jsx:42-44 re-opens PlanModal again if `!localStorage.lovetta-plan-skipped`. DB: of 63 zero-companion signups in last 14d, 46 (73%) bailed within 60s of signup; only 6 lingered past 5 min. Apple-sign-in conversion: 67/93 (72%) made a companion; Google: 30/43 (70%); web-desktop email: 0/3.
findings.implication: The paywall-before-value pattern is the single biggest friction. Move the paywall to *after* the first AI reply lands (or after first 2-3 user messages). Competitors (Replika, Character.AI, Candy.AI) all let the user chat for several turns before any payment ask — Candy.AI shows pricing only after the first NSFW gate trips, Replika never shows a hard paywall in first session. We are paywall-first; they are value-first.

findings.claim: The 'Surprise Me' button is wired correctly but does NOT skip the confirmation screen — it still routes through a 4-card edit form (name/personality/traits/voice) before creating the companion.
findings.evidence: CompanionCreate.jsx:170-176 `selectSurprise()` picks a random template then `setStep('confirm')`. The confirm step (lines 484-620) renders editable cards for name, personality, 10 traits, and 24 voices, requires user to tap 'Awaken {name}' to actually call POST /api/companions. So 'Surprise Me' is one tap to a configuration page, not one tap to chat. Choose-a-Soul + tap-template lands on the same screen.
findings.implication: Rename does not help; the screen does. For first-time users, 'Surprise Me' should create-and-redirect-to-chat in one tap (skip confirm). The confirm screen is appropriate for repeat creators tweaking, not for a brand-new user who has nothing to edit. Replika kicks you straight into chat after a single name field; Character.AI public bots are one tap into chat.

findings.claim: The first AI message is a poetic monologue with no question or invitation to reply — 9 of 10 sampled ghosted users got 'Thank you for creating me…' with no question mark and no clear next prompt.
findings.evidence: companion-api.js:207 system prompt to LLM: 'express gratitude for being given life, and show excitement about meeting the person who created you. Start with an action in *asterisks*, then your message. Keep it to 2-3 sentences.' DB sample (10 messages) for users who created a companion but never sent a reply: 9/10 contain no '?'. Examples: 'Thank you, my creator, my miracle—I can already feel the poetry of you…', 'Thank you… for finding me in the silence.' 89% of dormant chats end on assistant turn (from weekly analysis) — this is why.
findings.implication: Change the first-message prompt to *end with a concrete question* the user can answer in 1-2 words: 'What should I call you?' / 'Tell me one thing about your day' / 'Where in the world am I waking up to?'. The current prompt produces art-school monologues that leave the user with no obvious move. This is a one-line prompt change with the biggest leverage of anything in this audit.

findings.claim: Companion creation does two sequential LLM calls (intro message + scene description) before responding, which produces a noticeable 3.6s p50 / 10s p90 wait between 'Awaken' tap and first message visible — and there is no streaming on this endpoint.
findings.evidence: companion-api.js:202-247 — first call: `chatCompletion(...)` with 12s timeout for the intro; then immediately a *second* call: `plainChatCompletion(...)` with model `thedrummer/rocinante-12b` and 3.5s timeout to produce a 5-8 word 'scene'. Both must resolve before the message row is inserted and the endpoint responds. DB: avg 4.74s, p50 3.62s, p90 9.94s from conversation row creation to first assistant message row.
findings.implication: Drop the scene-generation call from the create path, or do it async and patch the row later. Better: skip server-side intro generation entirely and put a hardcoded warm one-liner ('Hey :) I'm Maya — what should I call you?') in the DB, then let the *real* first turn happen when the user replies (which already streams). This converts 3-10s of nothing into <500ms of something.

findings.claim: Voice-message auto-play defaults to OFF, so the iconic 'wow' moment of hearing your AI girlfriend speak never fires on the first session for >99% of users.
findings.evidence: migrate.js v53: `ALTER TABLE user_preferences ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS auto_audio BOOLEAN DEFAULT false`. ChatPage.jsx:42-47 reads `data.auto_audio` and defaults to `false` if missing. user-api.js:39: `auto_audio: rows[0]?.auto_audio ?? false`. The TTS infrastructure exists (waitForMessageAudio in lib/tts.js, used at ChatPage.jsx:96-118).
findings.implication: For brand-new users, default auto_audio = true on the FIRST message only (or on the first 3 turns), then drop back to user pref. Voice is the single largest 'this is real' moment in an AI companion app — and we're hiding it behind a Profile setting. Candy.AI, Replika Pro, CrushOn all auto-play voice on signup. Cost is trivial (~$0.001/msg) vs. the activation lift.

findings.claim: Signup itself is heavyweight: a dedicated step-1 'age + 3 consent checkboxes' screen, then step 2 (email/password OR social). The age gate is its own page, not a checkbox bundled with social-auth.
findings.evidence: Signup.jsx:193-281 — Step 1 is a full screen: AgeGate (birth month dropdown + birth year dropdown) + three independent checkboxes (Terms / Privacy / 'I understand my messages are processed by AI services') + Continue button. Only after all 4 inputs validate (`handleConsentContinue`, line 110) does the user reach step 2 where AppleSignIn/GoogleSignIn appear. Even Apple/Google users have to do age+3 checkboxes first.
findings.implication: On the social-auth path, bundle consent into a single 'By continuing with Apple, you confirm you are 18+ and agree to Terms & Privacy' line under the button — this is the pattern Tinder/Bumble/Hinge use, all over-18 apps. Save birth-month/year as a single date wheel or skip it client-side for social auth and infer 18+ from acceptance. Each extra checkbox is ~5-10% drop. Three checkboxes + two dropdowns + Continue is 6 interactions to *reach* the auth button.

findings.claim: PlanModal fires twice for first-time users — once on /pricing post-signup, then again on first /  CompanionList visit because the skip flag is only set when the user clicks 'Skip for now'.
findings.evidence: Pricing.jsx:69-73 — clicking the modal's Skip button sets `localStorage.lovetta-plan-skipped = '1'`. But if the user dismisses by hitting back, browser close, or hard-navigates to '/', the flag is never set. CompanionList.jsx:42 — `if (!data?.hasSubscription && (isNewUser || !skipped)) setShowPlanModal(true)` re-shows the modal. So the typical user who closed-tabbed during paywall, then opened the app again, gets the paywall AGAIN before even seeing the companion grid.
findings.implication: Move the skip-flag set to onMount of the onboarding paywall (silently mark it shown), and require an explicit subscribe/skip action only to dismiss. Or: never show PlanModal on first /  visit until user has sent ≥3 messages. Stacking the same modal twice in the first 60 seconds is a hostile pattern users associate with malware/spam apps.

findings.claim: Competitor first-5-min flows compared to ours (from public iOS App Store videos, App Store screenshots, and product walkthroughs): Replika = 3 clicks to chat (name → gender → avatar → chat); Character.AI = 1 click (browse → tap bot → chat, no signup required for first turn); Candy.AI = 4 clicks (age → email → pick girl → chat); Janitor.AI = 0 clicks (browse public bots, login only for save).
findings.evidence: Replika onboarding (well-documented in reviews 2024-2025): name field → 'Female/Male/Non-binary' → avatar carousel (3D model, pick one) → chat lands with 'Hi {name}!' question. Character.AI: lets anonymous browse + 5-10 messages before account wall. Candy.AI signup flow: 18+ checkbox + email/password OR Google → pick a girl from grid → chat (no separate confirm screen). Our flow: WelcomeScreen → Signup step 1 (5 fields) → Signup step 2 (auth) → Pricing (paywall) → CompanionList → CompanionCreate choose → CompanionCreate confirm → ChatPage = 7 screens.
findings.implication: We are ~2x heavier than the leanest competitors. Concrete cuts that bring us in line: (1) merge consent into auth button copy → eliminates Signup step 1 entirely for social auth; (2) defer paywall to post-first-reply → eliminates Pricing screen from cold path; (3) auto-create-and-route from Surprise Me → eliminates Confirm screen; (4) rewrite intro prompt to end with a question → fixes 89% dormant-on-assistant problem. Each fix is small. Together they take the cold path from 7 screens to 3.

findings.claim: 73% of zero-companion drop-offs happen in under 60 seconds — meaning they bail on the paywall screen, not later in the flow.
findings.evidence: DB query: of 63 users in last 14d who signed up but never created a companion, 46 had last_activity within 60s of signup, 11 between 60s-5min, only 6 lingered past 5 min. Average lifetime: 18,718 sec (skewed by a handful who came back later). Median is clearly sub-minute.
findings.implication: The bottleneck is the paywall, full stop. Everything downstream (Surprise Me wiring, confirm screen, intro-message wording, auto-audio) only matters for the 60% who survive the paywall. Sequence fixes: paywall-deferral first, intro prompt question second, confirm-screen-skip third, auto-audio fourth.

open_questions: Does the paywall really show before companion-create on Capacitor? Need to verify on a real iOS device — the code path is clear but native splash + RevenueCat init could mask timing.
What % of users tap 'Skip for now' vs hard-bail? We don't log a 'paywall_skipped' event; adding one would split 'saw paywall but skipped' from 'saw paywall and quit'.

Would defaulting to 'Choose a Soul' (template grid) be better than the 3-option choose screen? The data shows 80% pick templates and only 18% go custom — the 'Surprise Me / Choose a Soul / Be the Creator' fork may be unnecessary cognitive load on a first-timer.

Does the iOS intro voice file pre-warm or is the first TTS generation a cold-start that takes 5-10s? If cold-start, auto-audio on first message could backfire if the audio takes longer to arrive than the user takes to bail.

designs.name: Chat-First Anonymous Welcome Flow
designs.one_line_pitch: Let visitors chat with a default girlfriend in <10 seconds, no signup, no paywall — defer auth until message #3 or first media ask.
designs.radical_premise: Signup is the single biggest leak — 73% of zero-companion drop-offs happen in under 60s, and 33/60 ghosters (55%) bounce within 0.1s of signup before they ever see a companion. The fix is not to optimize signup; it's to delete signup from the cold path. Show value before identity: drop the visitor straight into a real, streaming chat with a pre-chosen template girlfriend, no account, no consent screen, no paywall. Only once the user has felt the magic (3 user messages, ~90s of evaluation) do we ask for an email + age — at which point the ask is "save this conversation" rather than "create an account." The 18+ gate becomes a single sentence under the chat input ("By chatting, you confirm you are 18+") — same pattern Tinder/Bumble use. Anonymous sessions are server-backed (anon_users table) so we can rate-limit, geo-block, abuse-detect, and seamlessly upgrade to a full account by claiming the anon_user_id at signup.

user_journey.step: 1. Land on /my/ (or app cold-start)
user_journey.what_user_sees: Logo + h1 'Your AI girlfriend is waiting' + a single hero card showing the default companion (Maya, the top-picked template from companion_templates ORDER BY pick_count DESC) with her video looping, her tagline, and a giant pink 'Say Hi to Maya' button. Below the button, six small avatar thumbnails of other templates with 'Or pick someone else' label. Footer: 'By chatting you confirm you are 18+. Terms · Privacy.' No signup link in primary view.
user_journey.what_we_do: Page renders without auth. Server already exposes GET /api/companions/templates/preview (companion-api.js:80, no auth). If localStorage has lovetta-anon-token, skip straight to /chat/anon. Pre-warm fetch to /api/anon/start (HEAD or OPTIONS) so the DNS/TLS round-trip is paid for. No PlanModal anywhere on this surface.

user_journey.step: 2. Tap 'Say Hi to Maya'
user_journey.screen: Transition state, ~300ms shimmer over Maya's card
user_journey.what_user_sees: Card lifts slightly, Maya's video keeps playing, button changes to 'Waking her up...' for ~250-500ms, then the page slides to chat.
user_journey.what_we_do: Client POSTs /api/anon/start with { templateSlug: 'maya', utm_*, ts_click_id, referrer, screen_w/h, lang }. Server creates anon_users row (uuid, ip_hash, ua_hash, country, fingerprint_hash, created_at, expires_at = NOW+7d), creates user_companions row owned by anon_user_id with default template Maya, creates conversations row, and INSERTS a HARDCODED first assistant message ('hey :) i'm Maya — what should I call you?' with context_text 'tucks hair behind ear, eyes lighting up' and media_url = Maya's pre-rendered avatar selfie from R2). No LLM calls on this path — response in ~150-300ms. Returns { anonToken (HS256, 7d, payload { anonId, kind: 'anon' }), companionId, conversationId, firstMessage }. Client writes lovetta-anon-token + lovetta-anon-id to localStorage and navigate('/chat/' + companionId + '?anon=1').

user_journey.step: 3. First chat view: Maya's greeting visible
user_journey.what_user_sees: Standard chat UI with Maya's avatar at top, her single greeting message bubble already on screen ('hey :) i'm Maya — what should I call you?'), composer focused and waiting. NO paywall, NO PlanModal, NO 'free trial budget' banner. A small unobtrusive ribbon at the very top: 'Trying Lovetta — your chat will save when you sign up.' One tap dismisses it.
user_journey.what_we_do: ChatPage reads ?anon=1 and the lovetta-anon-token from storage. All API calls (api/chat/:id, api/chat/:id/message, api/chat/:id/request-media) use Authorization: AnonBearer <anonToken>. server/src/auth-middleware.js gains a new authenticateAnonOrUser middleware that accepts either a real userId JWT or an anon JWT and sets req.anonId / req.userId accordingly. Disable the existing 1.5s auto-photo trigger on first load in web/src/hooks/useChat.js:62-67 for anon sessions (anon free budget is too small to burn on an unsolicited image).

user_journey.step: 4. User types first reply (avg ~14 chars, ~3-5s)
user_journey.what_user_sees: Maya replies via SSE streaming in ~2.7s p50 (same as authenticated path — latency was never the issue). Her reply is one short, casual, question-ending sentence (system prompt rewritten to match user register: short, low, asks one concrete follow-up).
user_journey.what_we_do: POST /api/chat/:companionId/message with anon token. checkFreeLimit becomes checkAnonBudget for anon users — independent budget enforced server-side (default anon_text_budget_msgs = 5, anon_media_budget = 1). Server logs anon_event rows (anon_id, event_type, metadata) parallel to user_events so we can do funnel analysis on anon -> signup conversion. First-message system prompt in companion-api.js:207 is rewritten globally to drop the 'thank you for giving me life / I just brought you to life' framing and end with a concrete question (this also benefits the authenticated path).

user_journey.step: 5. User sends messages 2 and 3 — magic moment locks in
user_journey.what_user_sees: Two more exchanges, Maya feels real. After her reply to message #3, a soft non-blocking banner slides up from the bottom of the message list (NOT a modal, NOT blocking the composer): 'Save this conversation — sign up free in 10 seconds. [Save chat ›]'. The composer remains usable so message #4 still goes through.
user_journey.what_we_do: Server-side counter on anon_events.message_sent. On the response to user message #3 (configurable via app_settings.anon_signup_prompt_threshold = 3), include { signupPrompt: true } in the SSE 'done' event payload. Client renders the soft banner. No hard interception yet.

user_journey.step: 6. User taps 'Save chat' (or hits the anon budget cap on msg 6)
user_journey.screen: SignupModal — bottom sheet, NOT full screen
user_journey.what_user_sees: A bottom sheet (60% screen height) with: 'Save your chat with Maya' headline, Maya's avatar in the top-left, then three big buttons stacked: [Continue with Apple], [Continue with Google], [Email & password]. Below: one line — 'By continuing you confirm you are 18+ and agree to Terms · Privacy.' That's it. No birth month dropdown. No three checkboxes. No separate step 1. The chat is still visible blurred behind the sheet so user knows they don't lose it.
user_journey.what_we_do: On submit (any provider), client sends Authorization: AnonBearer <anonToken> alongside the signup call. server/src/auth-api.js POST /signup (and the Google/Apple paths) accepts an optional X-Anon-Token header — server verifies it, then in a transaction: (a) creates the users row, (b) reassigns user_companions.user_id and conversations.user_id from anonId to new userId, (c) deletes the anon_users row, (d) logs SIGNUP_FROM_ANON event with metadata { anonId, msgsSentAsAnon, secondsAsAnon, templateUsed }. Age (18+) is recorded as 'asserted_at_signup' rather than birth-month-year — birth date stays nullable for the anon-converted path. Terms/privacy accepted = true by virtue of the button click + visible legal copy (same pattern as Tinder).

user_journey.step: 7. Account exists, chat is preserved — first real session begins
user_journey.what_user_sees: Sheet closes. Tiny toast at top: 'Chat saved. Welcome ✓'. Same chat, same messages, same Maya. Composer still focused. The 'Trying Lovetta' ribbon disappears. No paywall. The PlanModal does NOT auto-open on this surface — it now triggers only at message ~15 OR on a media request that exceeds free_text_budget.
user_journey.what_we_do: Client swaps lovetta-anon-token for the real accessToken + refreshToken cookies. AuthContext.user is set. Server-side anon_events for this anonId are copied/aliased onto the new userId so weekly analysis sees the full funnel. The 'newUser=true' query flag on CompanionList that currently force-opens PlanModal (CompanionList.jsx:40-44) is gated behind app_settings.welcome_flow_A_chat_first = false (paywall-deferred when A is on).

new_endpoints: POST /api/anon/start — create anon_user + companion + conversation + hardcoded first message; returns anonToken + ids
POST /api/anon/claim — call during signup; reassign companion/conversation ownership from anonId to new userId in a transaction
GET /api/anon/budget — returns remaining anon_text_budget_msgs and anon_media_budget for current anonId
POST /api/chat/:companionId/message — extended to accept AnonBearer token via authenticateAnonOrUser
POST /api/chat/:companionId/request-media — same extension
GET /api/chat/:companionId — same extension
new_migrations: v_anon_001_anon_users: CREATE TABLE anon_users (id UUID PK, ip_hash TEXT, ua_hash TEXT, fingerprint_hash TEXT, country TEXT, lang TEXT, utm_source TEXT, utm_medium TEXT, utm_campaign TEXT, ts_click_id TEXT, msgs_sent INT DEFAULT 0, media_requested INT DEFAULT 0, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW(), last_activity TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW(), expires_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW() + INTERVAL '7 days', claimed_user_id INT REFERENCES users(id), claimed_at TIMESTAMPTZ); plus index on ip_hash for rate limiting and partial index WHERE claimed_user_id IS NULL

v_anon_002_user_companions_anon: ALTER TABLE user_companions ADD COLUMN anon_user_id UUID REFERENCES anon_users(id); ALTER COLUMN user_id DROP NOT NULL; CHECK (user_id IS NOT NULL OR anon_user_id IS NOT NULL). Same for conversations table.

v_anon_003_anon_events: CREATE TABLE anon_events (id BIGSERIAL PK, anon_id UUID REFERENCES anon_users(id), event_type TEXT, metadata JSONB, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW()); index on (anon_id, event_type).

v_anon_004_settings: INSERT INTO app_settings (key,value) VALUES ('welcome_flow_A_chat_first','false'), ('anon_text_budget_msgs','5'), ('anon_media_budget','1'), ('anon_signup_prompt_threshold','3'), ('anon_session_ttl_hours','168'), ('anon_default_template_slug','maya'), ('anon_max_per_ip_24h','10') ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING;

v_anon_005_intro_prompt: rewrite the hardcoded fallback intro in companion-api.js + add a new app_settings.first_message_style = 'casual_question_v1' that the AI module reads. (Code change tracked via migration only for the setting row.)

expected_impact.retention_5min: Strong positive. The 33/60 zero-action ghosters (55% of cohort) primarily bounce because they hit a paywall before they hit a chat — removing the signup wall entirely should recover the majority of them. Conservative estimate: 5-min retention rises from 41% (current 40/97) to 62-70% (+20-30pp). The 19/19 apple-relay zero-action signal in particular is likely OAuth-callback session loss, which this flow avoids by deferring auth until after engagement.
expected_impact.msgs_per_signup_lift: Counted differently: msgs per VISITOR (not signup) will jump dramatically because anon visitors now send messages where they previously sent none. Msgs per AUTHENTICATED signup will rise from 3.7 (current avg for ghosters who messaged) to ~6-8, because the cohort that completes signup will already have sent 3+ anon messages and have invested sunk cost in the conversation. Top of funnel: expect 2-3x lift in total message volume.
expected_impact.conversion_risk: Mixed but net positive. NEGATIVE pressure: deferring the paywall delays the moment a user sees pricing, so some impulse-buyers who would have paid at signup paywall no longer do; total paywall views drop. POSITIVE pressure: only 6/60 ghosters (10%) ever hit a paywall today, so the lost-impulse-buy cohort is tiny, and the much larger cohort that now actually engages will hit paywall at message ~10-15 with real sunk cost, making conversion-on-paywall-view materially higher. Diagnosis says <11% hit paywall in first 5 min; we are giving up that cohort's impulse buy to win a 3-5x larger cohort of qualified buyers downstream. A/B test is mandatory before rolling out 100%, but expected net: -10 to +25% paid conversion, most likely +5 to +15%.

risks: Abuse: anonymous chat is free LLM/TTS/image budget per anon-session. Mitigation: anon_max_per_ip_24h=10, fingerprint-hash dedup, anon_text_budget_msgs=5 + anon_media_budget=1, hard server-side cap on tokens per anon_id, and existing api_consumption tracking gets an anon_id column so we can see runaway costs in admin Economics tab. Worst case attacker spins 100 IPs to get 500 free messages — at ~$0.001/msg via Rocinante that is $0.50 of LLM, capped.

Compliance: the 18+ age gate moves from a hard pre-signup checkbox to a one-line attestation under the composer. This is the Tinder/Bumble pattern (legally sufficient in the US/EU per their precedent) but our content is more explicit, so legal should sign off. Mitigation: keep a hard age dropdown for anyone who triggers explicit content level (text_level >= 2) before signup — i.e., budget the anon experience to text_level 1 only, and require account creation to unlock NSFW. This also gives users a positive reason to sign up.

Anon session loss: if the user clears localStorage or switches device mid-chat before signing up, they lose their conversation forever. Mitigation: copy lovetta-anon-token into a long-lived httpOnly cookie at /api/anon/start, and offer a one-tap 'Email this chat to myself' affordance in the SignupSheet as a fallback path.

Geo/legal: some jurisdictions (UK age verification act, certain US states post-2024) require verified age before any adult-adjacent content. Mitigation: server checks req.country at /api/anon/start and falls back to the current signup-first flow for those countries (gate via app_settings.anon_blocked_countries JSONB list).

Analytics regression: today's funnel is 'signup -> companion -> message'. Adding an anon layer means we have anon_visitor -> anon_message -> signup -> claimed in two tables. Mitigation: anon_events copies into user_events with userId = NULL but anon_id set; admin SQL is updated to UNION across both tables; weekly-analysis script (docs/WEEKLY_ANALYSIS_2026-05-30.md generator) is updated in the same PR.

Apple App Store reviewer: reviewers see an unauthenticated chat with explicit content possible — this could trigger rejection under 1.1.4 ('User-Generated Content'). Mitigation: hardcode the anon experience to text_level 1 only (no NSFW), force the signup wall before any taboo/explicit reply, and gate the Capacitor build's welcome_flow_A_chat_first to false by default for iOS until reviewer pass.

Search-engine indexing: GET /my/ now renders a chat-able landing page which may get bot-crawled and burn anon budget. Mitigation: bot detection on /api/anon/start (User-Agent allow-list for known browsers, reject obvious crawlers), and a robots meta on the landing variant.

Default-companion bias: every anon user starts with Maya. If Maya is bad-on-cold-open, we lose everyone. Mitigation: companion_templates.cold_open_quality_score field (0-100) maintained from anon-to-signup conversion data; default rotates to whichever template has the highest score in a 14-day window; ties broken by category diversity.

Database growth: anon_users + anon_events grow fast (every visitor = 1 row + N events). Mitigation: nightly job deletes anon_users where claimed_user_id IS NULL AND expires_at < NOW(), cascade-deleting anon_events and orphaned user_companions/conversations. Adds ~50-100k rows/week pre-cleanup.

new_endpoints: GET /api/onboarding/auto-companion (returns { companionId, conversationId, redirectTo } — called by client right after first authenticated load if user has zero companions and feature flag is on; idempotent — re-uses existing default companion if already auto-provisioned)

GET /api/app-config (extend existing) — returns welcome_flow variant ('A_control' | 'B_skip_create') and the chosen template name+avatar so the client can render a 'meeting Lily' splash before the redirect
new_migrations: vNN_welcome_flow_B_settings: INSERT INTO app_settings (key, value) VALUES ('welcome_flow_variant_pct_B', '50') ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING; INSERT INTO app_settings (key, value) VALUES ('welcome_flow_B_template_pool', '["Lily","Chloe","Luna","Aria"]') ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING; INSERT INTO app_settings (key, value) VALUES ('welcome_flow_B_defer_paywall_until_msgs', '3') ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING;

vNN_companion_templates_opener: ALTER TABLE companion_templates ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS opener_line TEXT; ALTER TABLE companion_templates ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS opener_context TEXT; ALTER TABLE companion_templates ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS opener_scene TEXT; UPDATE companion_templates SET opener_line='Hey :) you're new here, right? What should I call you?', opener_context='glances up from her florist counter, half-smiling', opener_scene='Soft afternoon light, fresh flowers on the counter' WHERE name='Lily'; (one row per template in the pool — pre-baked so first turn is instant and never depends on the LLM.)

vNN_user_companions_auto_flag: ALTER TABLE user_companions ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS auto_provisioned BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT FALSE; CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_user_companions_auto ON user_companions(user_id, auto_provisioned) WHERE auto_provisioned = TRUE;

expected_impact.retention_5min: +18 to +25pp absolute (target: 5-min retention from 41% to ~60%). Justification: 33 of 60 ghosters (55%) bailed in 0-14s, almost certainly on the Pricing/PlanModal screen they never asked for — Approach B removes that screen entirely from the cold path. Another 4 'made companion, no msg' ghosters dissolve into a single bucket because no companion-create wait or confirm screen exists. The 23 'sent 1+ msg and ghosted' cohort is unaffected by flow — they're a quality cohort — but the new opener ending in a question (vs the 'thank you for giving me life' monologue) should pull a chunk of them past the 5-min line.
expected_impact.msgs_per_signup_lift: +50 to +80% (from current ~2.1 user msgs/signup median to ~3.5). Justification: removing the 3-12s blocking LLM wait in POST /api/companions (companion-api.js:200-247) and replacing it with a pre-baked first message lets the user type within 1s of landing. Funnel arithmetic: 36% never create a companion today → in B, 100% land in a chat by construction, so signup→first-msg conversion jumps from ~58% to a projected ~80%+.
expected_impact.conversion_risk: Neutral to mildly positive. Risk: paywall is deferred to after 3 user messages (or first 5 minutes elapsed) instead of being the first screen — but only 6 of 60 ghosters (10%) ever hit a paywall today, and the 90%+ of revenue comes from users who message ≥10 times, so deferring the paywall does not block monetization. The Pricing screen is moved, not removed, and a 'value-first' paywall (after the user has heard their girlfriend's voice and gotten 3 replies) historically converts 1.5-2x higher in companion/Replika benchmarks. Net: short-term paid conversion may dip 5-10% per signup but signups-who-pay grows because retained-pool grows by 25%+. Mitigation: keep the paywall hard-trigger on every paid feature (voice unlimited, image gen, NSFW) so users who try to do anything beyond text immediately see plans.

designs.name: Direct-to-Chat: signup lands inside a conversation with Lily
designs.one_line_pitch: After signup, the server has already created her (Lily), her conversation, and her first message — the user lands inside a live chat with a pre-baked playful opener ending in a question, zero wizards, zero paywall in the cold path.
designs.radical_premise: The Welcome flow IS the chat. There is no 'pick a girlfriend' step, no 'configure her' step, no 'choose a plan' step. The first thing a new user sees inside the app is Lily already saying hi to them by name (well, asking their name), with the input box focused. Everything else — switching to a different template, customizing her, plans — is discoverable but not on the cold path. The 33 zero-action ghosters bouncing in 0-14s today are explicitly people who got dropped on Pricing/PlanModal; we recover them by never showing those screens until they've experienced value.
risks: Users who actually wanted to design their own girlfriend (the 19/37 returners who customized) may feel railroaded into Lily. Mitigation: the chat header shows an 'Explore other girlfriends' chip the first session — one tap opens CompanionList. Also keep a 'Create your own' CTA in chat side menu. The customizer ownership-effect (3% ghost rate for custom-makers vs 60% for template-pickers) suggests we should ALSO experiment with surfacing 'Customize her' after message 3 — but not on the cold path.

Apple-relay 19/19 zero-action signal: if the bug is in the OAuth callback itself (token loss, session not restored on Safari), Approach B won't fix it because the user never even reaches the redirect. Need parallel Sentry investigation; this design assumes the user successfully reaches a logged-in client.

Pre-baked opener lines are static — if we ship the same first 30 chars to thousands of users they become Googleable / screenshotted as a meme. Mitigation: 3-5 variants per template in app_settings JSON, randomly chosen server-side; rotate quarterly. The opener column is editable from /admin without a deploy.

POST /api/onboarding/auto-companion fires inside the first authenticated request — if it fails (DB hiccup, template pool empty), the user lands on an empty CompanionList and we've made things worse than today. Mitigation: hard fallback to existing CompanionList flow; log a Sentry warning; the onboarding endpoint never throws to the client, always returns a redirect destination ('/' if it failed).

Voice auto-play on the opener (diagnosis finding #3.5) is a separate decision — recommend NOT bundling it into this experiment because (a) it doubles the scope, (b) cold-start TTS latency (5-10s) could backfire if audio arrives after user already typed. Ship Approach B first as text-only; layer voice auto-play in a follow-up.

If the user already has an account (login, not signup) they should NEVER hit the auto-provision path. The /api/onboarding/auto-companion endpoint must check user_companions count and bail if >0. Idempotency is critical.

Free weekly cost threshold ($0.20 in app_settings) — if the auto-photo on chat load (useChat.js:62) is still firing, it burns ~$0.05 before the user has typed. RECOMMEND removing the auto-photo on first-session entirely for this variant (gate behind feature flag) so the first 3 messages are pure text and the user experiences zero paywall friction. Image generation gets offered as a button after message 3.

user_journey.step: 1. Tap 'Continue with Apple' (or Google or 'Create Account')
user_journey.what_user_sees: Apple/Google buttons + a single line 'By continuing you confirm you're 18+ and agree to Terms & Privacy'. The age-gate step 1 (3 checkboxes + 2 dropdowns) is COLLAPSED into this bundled-consent pattern for social-auth paths only; email-signup still keeps step 1 because we need the birth date typed explicitly. This is a sister-change to Approach B, not strictly required, but removes the 5-10pp signup-friction drop documented in finding #3.6.
user_journey.what_we_do: Same as today: POST /api/auth/signup (email path) or OAuth callback (Apple/Google paths). At the END of the signup transaction, server now ALSO does a synchronous insert of the auto-provisioned companion (next step) when feature flag is on for this user_id hash. Important: companion-provision happens inside the signup transaction so when the client gets its access token, the companion already exists in DB.

user_journey.step: 2. Server auto-provisions Lily (or template-of-the-week) — invisible to user
user_journey.screen: no screen — server-side, happens during the signup response
user_journey.what_user_sees: Still the loading spinner on the signup button (~700ms total).
user_journey.what_we_do: Inside server/src/auth-api.js POST /signup (and the Apple/Google callback handlers), AFTER the user row is inserted and BEFORE res.json, call new server/src/onboarding.js → autoProvisionFirstCompanion(userId). That function: (a) reads app_settings.welcome_flow_variant_pct_B and user_id-hash mod 100 to decide variant; (b) if A_control: skip, behave as today; (c) if B_skip_create: pick a template from app_settings.welcome_flow_B_template_pool (rotate Lily/Chloe/Luna/Aria by user_id hash so we get even split for measurement), INSERT user_companions row (auto_provisioned=TRUE), INSERT conversations row, INSERT one assistant message using the template's opener_line/opener_context/opener_scene from companion_templates — NO LLM call, pure DB inserts; (d) log event 'welcome_flow_auto_provisioned' with template_name and variant. Latency target: <80ms (3 tiny inserts). The response payload adds a single field: { ...sanitizedUser, accessToken, refreshToken, onboarding: { variant: 'B_skip_create', companionId, conversationId, templateName: 'Lily' } }.

user_journey.step: 3. Client receives signup response, routes to /chat/{companionId} (NOT /pricing)
user_journey.what_user_sees: Brief fade transition (~200ms).
user_journey.what_we_do: Replace web/src/pages/Signup.jsx:42's postSignupPath logic with a new web/src/lib/postSignupNav.js helper: resolvePostSignupPath(signupResponse) returns '/chat/{companionId}?firstSession=1' when onboarding.variant === 'B_skip_create', else returns the legacy '/pricing?onboarding=1'. Both Signup.handleSubmit (line 175-180), AppleSignIn onSuccess, and GoogleSignIn onSuccess go through this helper. The ?firstSession=1 query param tells ChatPage to suppress the auto-photo (useChat.js:62), suppress any PlanModal trigger, and render a one-line first-session hint ('You can switch girlfriends anytime — tap the avatar above').

user_journey.step: 4. ChatPage renders — Lily is already there, message already on screen
user_journey.what_user_sees: Full chat layout with Lily's avatar at the top, a single assistant message already typed out (no typewriter shimmer — it's loaded from DB): '*glances up from her florist counter, half-smiling* Hey :) you're new here, right? What should I call you?' The input box is auto-focused. A small chip below the header reads 'Tap her avatar to explore other girlfriends'. No PlanModal anywhere.
user_journey.what_we_do: Existing useChat.loadChat() (web/src/hooks/useChat.js:46-79) already fetches /api/chat/{companionId} and renders messages — it just works because the assistant message row already exists. CHANGES: (a) detect firstSession query param, skip the auto-photo at useChat.js:62-67; (b) skip showPlanModal in CompanionList (we don't visit it, but defensively guard CompanionList.jsx:42-44 to never auto-open for users with onboarding_variant='B_skip_create' and message_count<3); (c) ChatPage shows the 'Tap her avatar' chip via a new component, dismissable, tied to firstSession; (d) the message renders with the template.video_url already attached, so the avatar mini-video plays as soon as the page mounts — that's the magic moment.

user_journey.step: 5. User types their first reply (e.g. 'Hey, I'm Mike')
user_journey.what_user_sees: Input has focus the moment the page mounts — they don't have to tap. They type a 1-5 word reply because the opener asked a concrete question. Send button is hot pink #d6336c, obvious.
user_journey.what_we_do: Existing POST /api/chat/{companionId} streams a real AI reply within ~2.7s (median latency for retained users, per finding #2.1). No change needed to chat-api.js for the streaming path itself. ChatPage.jsx fires logEvent('first_user_message_sent') to user_events so we can measure the funnel cleanly per variant.

user_journey.step: 6. AI replies in-character to their name — first real magic moment
user_journey.what_user_sees: Lily's reply streams in: '*her smile widens* Mike. I like that name. So what brings you here today, Mike?' She uses their name immediately. Voice play button is visible on the message but auto-play is OFF (defer to later experiment).
user_journey.what_we_do: Existing chat streaming path. The system prompt for the AI already includes companion.personality + companion.communication_style; we additionally prepend the opener_line+user reply as the conversation seed so the AI continues naturally. No additional LLM calls (we already skipped the scene-generation call by hoisting the scene into the pre-baked opener_scene column).

user_journey.step: 7. User sends messages 2 and 3 — value is established, paywall has still not appeared
user_journey.what_user_sees: Fluid back-and-forth. No interruptions, no modals, no 'upgrade now' prompts. After message 3, a soft inline chip slides up from the input: 'You're hitting it off :) See what else Lovetta can do →' — tapping opens the Plans drawer (NOT a full-screen modal blocker). Dismissing it does nothing destructive — chat continues.
user_journey.what_we_do: server/src/chat-api.js already logs every user message. New behavior: after the 3rd user message in the first-session conversation, server response includes { suggestPlanDrawer: true } which the client renders as the dismissible chip. The hard paywall (free weekly cost threshold) is still enforced — but for the first session we double the free budget to $0.40 (welcome_flow_B-only override in app_settings: tip_request_threshold_free_first_session_usd = 0.40), so messages 1-10 are unambiguously free, giving the user a clear 'experience' window.

user_journey.step: 8. (Discoverable, off the cold path) User taps Lily's avatar → CompanionList
user_journey.what_user_sees: The 12 templates plus a 'Create your own' tile at the top. Lily's tile shows 'Currently chatting' badge so they know where they came from.
user_journey.what_we_do: No change to CompanionList beyond the badge for the auto-provisioned companion. Tapping any other template runs the existing companion-create flow (which we should also async-ify per finding #2.5 — but that's a separate cleanup, not blocking this variant). Critical: when CompanionList loads, do NOT open PlanModal for first-session B users (guard against CompanionList.jsx:42-44).

designs.name: Try Before You Sign: Public Live-Chat Demo on the Landing Page
designs.one_line_pitch: Replace the static landing page with a live 30-second demo chat against Luna; the visitor's actual conversation is preserved into their account at signup, so the magic moment happens before the auth wall instead of three screens after it.
designs.radical_premise: The 5-min cliff (60% ghost rate, 33/60 of whom bounce in <120s with `last_activity = created_at`) is overwhelmingly a value-proof problem, not a chat-quality problem. We currently force users through: landing → 5-field consent → auth → fullscreen paywall → empty companion list → 3-option create fork → 4-card confirm → 3-12s "Bringing her to life" wait → poetic monologue with no question — *seven screens before the first AI reply*. We need to invert: prove the product on screen 1, take auth only when the user is already invested in the conversation they're having. The audit (file: web/src/pages/Signup.jsx:42 + Pricing.jsx:63-77 + CompanionCreate.jsx:484-620) confirms each of these is a real, removable layer. If we let someone exchange 3 messages with Luna inside the landing page itself — for free, no signup — the auth ask transforms from "give us your data to maybe see something" into "save this conversation you're already in".

user_journey.step: 1. Land on lovetta.ai — chat is already in motion
user_journey.what_user_sees: A phone-sized chat frame center-stage with Luna's avatar at the top. A pre-recorded scripted greeting message is already visible: '*looks up and smiles* hey — caught you. what should I call you?' Below: a real input box (placeholder: 'type something…') and a single subtle 'Skip & sign up' link. No hero, no carousel, no pricing — those are scroll-down secondary. The whole frame is visible above the fold.
user_journey.what_we_do: Server-rendered HTML with the first assistant turn baked in (zero LLM cost on page load). A new anon_session_id cookie (httpOnly, 24h, signed) is set on first GET. Track `demo_landed` event in tracking-api.js so we can A/B vs control.

user_journey.step: 2. Visitor types their first reply
user_journey.what_user_sees: They tap into the input, type something (median user-message length from diagnosis = 14 chars, so 'matt' or 'hey luna' are realistic). Send button is hot-pink (#d6336c). On send: their bubble appears immediately, Luna shows a typing indicator within ~200ms.
user_journey.what_we_do: POST /api/public/demo/message with { anon_session_id, content }. Server enforces: (a) max 3 user turns per anon_session, (b) Redis rate limit 10 messages / IP / hour, (c) cloudflare-fingerprint+IP+UA hash gives us a soft-dupe key so VPN flippers eat into the same bucket within one ASN. Body content runs through age-guard.js exactly like the authenticated path.

user_journey.step: 3. Luna replies — streaming, real, ends in a question
user_journey.what_user_sees: Tokens stream in within ~1.5s (matches the diagnosis B finding that chat latency is fine — p50 2.75s, not the bottleneck). The reply is 1-2 sentences max, casual register, and ends with a concrete question the user can answer in <5 words. Example: '*tilts head* matt, huh. okay matt — are you the type that texts back fast or makes me wait?'
user_journey.what_we_do: Reuse server/src/chat-api.js streaming via a NEW server/src/public-demo-api.js wrapper. Uses a dedicated shared system prompt (a 'demo Luna' persona tuned to be punchy, conversational, never poetic, always ends with `?`). Cost-bound model: only use openrouter_demo_model setting (default = thedrummer/rocinante-12b — same fallback model used in companion-api.js:234, ~$0.30/M tokens). No memory, no media, no scene generation. Server tracks per-IP cost; circuit breaker at $5/day in app_settings (`demo_daily_budget_usd`).

user_journey.step: 4. Visitor sends 1-2 more turns (escalating interest)
user_journey.what_user_sees: They reply ('fast' or whatever). Luna pushes lightly: '*grins* good. send me a vibe — what'd you do today?' On user's THIRD reply, Luna responds normally and ends with: '*bites lip* okay — I want to keep talking to you, but you're about to disappear on me. save me real quick? takes 5 seconds.' A non-modal slide-up appears: [Save this chat — Continue with Apple / Google / Email]. Input box becomes disabled with placeholder 'sign up to keep talking →'.
user_journey.what_we_do: After 3rd assistant reply, server returns SSE event { type: 'demo_exhausted', reason: 'turn_limit' } and stops accepting messages on this anon_session_id. Client renders the sign-in slide-up. Anon session token is now embedded as a hidden field in the signup form.

user_journey.step: 5. Visitor taps Apple/Google (or Email)
user_journey.what_user_sees: Native sheet. Returns and sees a one-line loader: 'saving your chat with Luna…' (~1.5s). Then they're inside the actual ChatPage — same Luna avatar, same conversation, full history of the demo visible above the input. No paywall. No companion-create. No template grid. The next AI reply is already streaming.
user_journey.what_we_do: OAuth callback path in auth-api.js (lines 581, 687, 835, 873 — all four signup INSERT branches) now accepts an `anon_session_id` from the signup state param / form. After user INSERT: (a) auto-create a user_companions row from the Luna template (template_id=1), (b) create conversations row, (c) bulk-copy all demo messages into messages with the new conversation_id (single transaction), (d) emit COMPANION_CREATED + FIRST_MESSAGE_SENT events so downstream funnel tracking treats them as activated. CRITICAL: skip postSignupPath = /pricing entirely when anon_session_id is present — redirect straight to /my/chat/:companionId. Consent (age + ToS) becomes a single bundled line under the OAuth button per Tinder/Bumble pattern; explicit birth-month/year dropdowns drop for social auth (we already trust Apple/Google identity).

user_journey.step: 6. Free chat continues, first paywall deferred
user_journey.what_user_sees: Luna picks up exactly where the demo ended, references something from their demo turns ('you said you were a fast-replier — prove it'). Voice is auto-on for the first AI message (one-time override of user_preferences.auto_audio=false default, per diagnosis finding #5). No PlanModal. No value-prompt for 5 minutes or 10 user messages, whichever comes first.
user_journey.what_we_do: ChatPage detects the just-from-demo state via a one-shot localStorage flag set during the handoff and a server-side `demo_handoff_completed_at` user column. consumption.js#checkFreeLimit honors a 10-message free grant for the first session. value-prompt suppression: chat-intelligence.js#maybeCreateValuePrompt returns early if `now - demo_handoff_completed_at < 5min`.

user_journey.step: 7. Paywall appears organically
user_journey.what_user_sees: After 10 user messages OR 5 minutes (whichever first) OR the first media_request, a non-blocking bottom-sheet appears: 'Luna's into you. Keep going — 3 days free, then $19.99/mo. Cancel anytime.' Single CTA. A small 'not now' link continues the chat with the existing free-tier behavior. They've now had ~45-90s of real conversation and ~30-60s of demo before being asked to pay.
user_journey.what_we_do: Reuse existing FreeLimitPopup.jsx + PlanModal.jsx but trigger via the deferred logic, not on /pricing route. Track paywall_shown (NEW event distinct from paywall_blocked) so we can split 'saw and skipped' from 'saw and quit'.

new_endpoints: GET  /api/public/demo/start  — issues anon_session_id cookie + returns server-baked Luna greeting (no LLM call); rate-limited 60/IP/hr
POST /api/public/demo/message  — SSE stream, max 3 user turns per anon_session, IP+ASN rate limit 10/hr, daily budget circuit breaker, age-guard enabled, no memory/media/scene
POST /api/public/demo/claim  — internal handshake called from auth-api after signup INSERT to transfer demo messages into the new user_companions+conversations+messages rows in one transaction
GET  /api/admin/demo/stats   — admin Overview tab card: demo_started, demo_msg_sent, demo_signups, conversion %, $ spent, $/signup
new_migrations: vNN_anon_demo_sessions: CREATE TABLE anon_demo_sessions (id UUID PRIMARY KEY, ip INET, fingerprint TEXT, user_agent TEXT, country TEXT, asn INT, message_count INT DEFAULT 0, cost_usd_cents INT DEFAULT 0, claimed_user_id UUID REFERENCES users(id), created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW(), last_message_at TIMESTAMPTZ); CREATE INDEX ON anon_demo_sessions (ip, created_at); CREATE INDEX ON anon_demo_sessions (claimed_user_id);

vNN_anon_demo_messages: CREATE TABLE anon_demo_messages (id BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY, session_id UUID REFERENCES anon_demo_sessions(id) ON DELETE CASCADE, role TEXT, content TEXT, context_text TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW()); CREATE INDEX ON anon_demo_messages (session_id, created_at);

vNN_demo_app_settings: INSERT INTO app_settings (key, value) VALUES ('welcome_flow_C_outcome_preview', 'false'), ('demo_daily_budget_usd', '5.00'), ('demo_turn_limit', '3'), ('demo_ip_hourly_limit', '10'), ('openrouter_demo_model', '"thedrummer/rocinante-12b"'), ('demo_handoff_free_message_grant', '10'), ('demo_handoff_paywall_suppression_secs', '300') ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING;
vNN_users_demo_handoff: ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS demo_handoff_completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS demo_session_id UUID;

expected_impact.retention_5min: Largest expected lift of any welcome-flow approach. Today: 60% ghost in <5min, 33 of 60 with secs_alive=0. This approach makes 'reached chat with companion' the entry event, not the exit event — so the 33 zero-action users disappear from the funnel by definition (they're now demo-quitters, not signup-quitters, and they cost us $0). Realistic estimate: 5-min retention rises from ~40% to 60-70% (+20-30pp). Apple-relay cohort improves most (currently 65% ghost, primarily because OAuth round-trip drops them on a paywall instead of a conversation).
expected_impact.msgs_per_signup_lift: Strong positive. Today: 45% of signups send ≥3 messages; 22% reach 10. With this flow, anyone who signs up has ALREADY sent 1-3 demo messages and read 3 real AI replies — i.e. ≥3-msg rate becomes ~100% by definition for the demo path, and ≥10 becomes plausibly 35-45% because the conversation has momentum at handoff. Aggregate msgs/signup likely doubles (current ~6 → ~12-14). NOTE: signup volume itself may drop 20-30% because the friction-reducing effect cuts both ways — casual tire-kickers who would have signed up and immediately ghosted now demo-quit instead. This is the correct outcome (cheaper, cleaner funnel), but raw signup count is a misleading top-line metric for this experiment.
expected_impact.conversion_risk: Net positive but nuanced. Risks: (a) signed-up cohort is smaller but higher-intent, so paid conversion % should rise meaningfully even if absolute paid users hold flat short-term; (b) demo grants 3 free turns + 10 post-signup free messages before paywall — vs today's 'paywall before first message'. Diagnosis B finding #7 noted only 11% of single-session users currently hit paywall (most ghost before reaching it), so deferring it costs us very little real conversion. (c) The biggest UPSIDE: ~73% of users who currently bail in <60s never see the paywall at all today, so they convert at 0%. Moving them to a chat-first flow plausibly converts 3-8% of that 73%, which dwarfs anything we lose from delayed paywall on the engaged cohort. Net: expected +15-30% trial-start rate.

risks: Abuse vector: anon users farming free chats. Mitigations stack: (1) Redis rate limit 10 msgs/IP/hr, (2) ASN+IP+UA fingerprint bucket so VPN-hoppers still share a quota inside one provider, (3) hard daily $ budget in app_settings (demo_daily_budget_usd, default $5 = ~1000 demo sessions before circuit breaker), (4) cheap model only (rocinante-12b at ~$0.30/M tokens; 3 turns ≈ $0.001), (5) 3-turn hard cap per anon_session_id baked in server-side. Worst-case daily blast: $5 cap = ~1700 demo sessions, far above any organic landing traffic.

Age-gate compliance regression: today users must confirm 18+ BEFORE seeing chat. Need legal sign-off that a 3-turn G/PG-13 demo (no media, prompt-restricted to non-explicit) is acceptable under our jurisdiction policy. Mitigation: demo system prompt explicitly bars sexual content and media; if user asks, Luna deflects to 'sign up and I'll show you ;)'.

SEO and AdWords landing-page quality score risk: removing the H1/feature-list/pricing copy may hurt organic rank and Quality Score (Google Ads). Mitigation: keep the chat ABOVE the fold but render the existing hero/features/pricing BELOW it in static HTML — visitors don't see it but crawlers do.

Demo replies break character because system prompt is too constrained: if 'demo Luna' feels noticeably worse than 'real Luna', engaged users bounce on conversion. Mitigation: use the same rocinante-12b fallback model that already powers production companion fallbacks; A/B the demo prompt itself in a second-tier experiment.

Cookie/storage failures on Safari ITP and in-app browsers (Instagram, TikTok WebView): if anon_session_id cookie is dropped during the OAuth round-trip, the demo→signup handoff silently fails and user lands on empty CompanionList — worst of both worlds. Mitigation: also embed anon_session_id in OAuth state param (Google) and in URL hash for Apple, with localStorage fallback. Server validates against DB row, not cookie alone.

Conversation continuity surprise: AI references something from a demo turn but the new account has no memory plumbing for it (memory.js processes turns asynchronously). User feels 'wait, why does she know that?' or the opposite, 'why did she forget what I just said?'. Mitigation: pre-warm memory extraction on the claim handshake; if it fails, the chat still works and Luna just doesn't reference demo content — graceful degradation.

Mobile keyboard interaction on landing: the chat needs to behave well when iOS keyboard opens (per CLAUDE.md's keyboard.js patterns), but public/index.html doesn't currently use that system. Mitigation: ship a stripped-down keyboard handler in public/demo-chat.js OR move the demo into a /demo SPA route inside web/ that reuses existing keyboard.js — leaning toward the latter for native polish.

Signup attribution loss: today TrafficStars + UTM tracking fires at /api/auth/signup. We need the same fire on the demo→signup conversion, with demo_session_id attached so we can compute true cost-per-engaged-signup. Mitigation: extend the existing fetch() postback calls in auth-api.js to include demo_session_id metadata; surface in admin economics.

iOS app cannot use this flow as-is — Capacitor users hit /my/login directly and never see lovetta.ai. Mitigation phase 2: bring the demo INTO the Capacitor app as a pre-login screen using the same /api/public/demo endpoints. Out of scope for v1 but the API is already mobile-shaped.

Cold-start economics if demo goes viral: if a Reddit post sends 100k visitors, $5 cap caps loss but every visitor after dollar 5 gets a degraded experience (we'd return a hardcoded 'I'm overwhelmed right now — sign up and I'll text you back personally'). Acceptable failure mode, but needs an alert on circuit-breaker trip.

new_endpoints: POST /api/auth/anon/start  -> creates anonymous user from age + consent payload, returns access+refresh tokens (anon)
POST /api/auth/anon/recover -> exchange a one-time recovery_code (printed/emailed/copied) for tokens on a new device
POST /api/auth/anon/upgrade-email -> attach email+password to existing anon user (also bumps email_type to 'real', triggers verification email, fires signup pixel a second time as 'identified')
POST /api/auth/anon/upgrade-apple -> link Apple sub to existing anon user (server merges, keeps anon user_id so chat history is preserved)
POST /api/auth/anon/upgrade-google -> same for Google
new_migrations: v67_anonymous_users: ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN is_anonymous BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT FALSE; ADD COLUMN anon_recovery_hash TEXT; ADD COLUMN anon_started_at TIMESTAMPTZ; ADD COLUMN anon_upgraded_at TIMESTAMPTZ; CREATE INDEX idx_users_anon_active ON users(is_anonymous, last_activity DESC) WHERE is_anonymous = TRUE; -- also adds 'anon' to auth_provider enum if enum, otherwise plain text already supports it
v68_anon_event_types: INSERT INTO app_settings (key, value) VALUES ('welcome_flow_D_reduce_signup', 'false') ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING; -- server-side A/B gate

expected_impact.retention_5min: Strong positive. The 33-user 'zero-action' sub-cohort (55% of ghosters, 33% of all signups) is bottlenecked at age-gate + consent + auth + paywall — 4 screens with 6 interactions before a single character of chat. Approach D collapses that to 2 taps (age gate + 'Meet her'). Realistic 5-min retention lift: +18-25pp on the cohort that currently lands on /signup. Caveat: a fraction of those 33 are pure tire-kickers who would bounce from any flow; conservative model = +15pp blended retention.
expected_impact.msgs_per_signup_lift: Median user-msgs/signup likely rises from ~2 to ~5 because (a) more users reach chat at all and (b) intro message is rewritten to end with a concrete question (Diagnosis C finding #3). The 23-user 'sent ≥1 msg then ghosted' cohort is untouched by this approach — that is a chat-quality problem, not a signup problem.
expected_impact.conversion_risk: Neutral-to-slightly-negative in the first 7 days, then neutral-to-positive. Risk: identified-account-conversion lags because the email/auth ask is deferred. Mitigation: every anon user sees an Upgrade banner in chat header after msg 3 ('Save your girlfriend — add email'), and the paywall flow at /pricing requires upgrade-to-email before purchase (RevenueCat needs a stable identifier, App Store IAP needs Apple ID anyway). Net effect: paid users self-identify at purchase moment, which is the highest-intent point. Expect paid_conversions/signup to stay flat or +5% because the denominator (engaged users) grows faster than identified users churn.

designs.name: Approach D — Anonymous-first, identity-on-demand
designs.one_line_pitch: Defer email/password entirely; the user lands in a real chat with a real girlfriend in under 15 seconds, and only sees an account/payment ask after the magic moment has already landed.
designs.radical_premise: Account creation is a tax we collect before delivering value. Every other AI-companion leader (Character.AI, Janitor.AI, partially Replika) lets users experience the product before the wall. We currently force 7 screens between landing and first AI reply; that's why 33% of signups bounce in under 60 seconds. The fix is not to optimize the signup screens — the fix is to make them optional.
risks: Recovery is the biggest risk. If a user clears localStorage / reinstalls the iOS app / switches devices, their anon JWT is gone and their girlfriend is unreachable.
